title: Albanian talking about modern Albanian history

transcript.text: The dictator who moved the country, what was his name?
He studied for some years in France,
Where he was kicked out of the university
He came here and he talked all the time about moral to us.
Fuck the minds of Albanians and so on.
When did he start his reign?
Lasted until, I mean, he died many years before they said he died.
He died and somebody was showing up all the time in the parliament,
Yeah, because they had to keep the people with the image, you know.
His image was really important for the people.
Eventually, they found out that he was dead.
Well, that he's dead now, they know,
But they haven't found out really.
The moment where they said, then where Hoca died,
Everybody in Albania had to cry.
It was really like kids at school, they had to cry.
If you can't cry, it's a problem.
He was like this big father.
So they pour, like, lemon juice on their eyes or something?
I think they kind of had to fight and then started crying.
But you cry if everybody's crying.
Or a lot of people happy when he died.
Because he's sort of the reason, he's the reason that Albania is so isolated.
The fact is that after he died, there was another guy,
Who called Ramizali, which was not as tough as he was.
So while we were the last country standing for communism,
He just had to connect this student revolution with the fact that,
Okay, now we switch to democracy.
And he left the power slowly.
And it's still, like, nowadays we do perceive this,
I call it the wave of communism.
It's based strongly on communism techniques.
The techniques of the party or the government?
And then the government itself now, it's, it's, I mean,
Mother communism, mother communist party had two children.
And they were the democrat party and the socialist party.
And the people in these parties all come from the communist party.
There are some new guys, but they are nothing.
I mean, the only person who is...
Because I noticed that they have, like, the democratic party flags, you said, all over the street.
Yes, and the democratic party was the party was ruling us at first.
So, so, 91, they started ruling the country and it was this mounted up reality.
And, uh, nobody had the concept of, uh, of, uh, capitalism or democracy or, uh, there was, uh, actually, there was a lot of, like, growth in the individual economy.
And, uh, before they had no property, then suddenly, they started growing up.
And, uh, afterwards, there was the 97 scandal, those pyramidal scams, they had to leave because it was just insane.
Then you had the socialists, which were obviously corrupted, but at least they had some kind of intellect, you know.
And then they had to leave because the Albanians, uh, while this guy, which is now our prime minister, which is the main guy, he, he said, uh, all the time in the parliament,
They are thieves and not fair and they are bad, bad, bad, bad.
Well, he, he looked to me really, really, really stupid and very silly, you know.
While he won the, the other elections and that was, like, a big shock for each mind, which had some kind of brain inside.
So now he's going on with this, with this really interesting performance and he's, like, uh, he's very much of the big speech and, yeah, yeah, friends with George Bush.
Actually, he decided once to give a part of the seat to Greece, imagine.
But then some, some guys from the EU, I think, they told him, you cannot do that, actually, that's out of any standard.
And he's, like, all right, then I take it back.
And now we are under his...
Now, ah, there is a new thing.
The fact is, the past elections, Democrats and Socialists were equal, almost.
So, there was another party inside, called LSI, social movement for integration, which actually decided to join with the Democrats, because so they could have the power together.
It was not enough votes for the socialists, so he could join them, I think, so he decided to go for the Democrats.
And finally, they are kind of keeping a certain balance, at least it's not totalitarian power, because the concept of the main guy of the Democrats, it's totalitarian power.
He's really, like, and there is no actual resistance in Albania, not from the people, not in the parliament.
The only guys who are actually...
Are they afraid of him, or they just don't care?
Also, you can be afraid of him.
Well, they don't care, it depends.
What's the name of it? LSI.
No, seriously, like, I took notes from the History Museum.
I'd like to learn about these things.
But each thing, I think each thing that happens in Albanian politics doesn't really matter.
There is this, talking about life in Albania, there is this very big potential of anarchists around, like, each thing is anarchist somehow.
You ain't got the presence of the state much.
Try to go up north, there is nothing like that.
You're still following some kind of traditional laws, deriving from canoe.
How do you spell the degree?

Yeah, when I entered Albania, it seemed like it was very traditional, like, you know, farmers working the fields, you know, little kids working the fields, lots of wild animals, and it was like, they don't even, they're living off the land.
It's really nice, this, actually, this so-called opportunity you have in Albania to reach that basic lifestyle and in between nature and with simple rules.

I mean, you can easily function in a community if you are a bit wise, you can have things all right, like, even educate the community itself, which is usually very close towards, I don't know, recycling or...
Yeah, there was no, I haven't seen litter here, I haven't seen litter, and there's litter all over the place in all the other countries in Europe.
In Tirana, you haven't seen litter?
Well, I don't think so, but not...
But not in the, not in the, not in, like, the countryside in Albania, I haven't seen it.
No, but in downtown Toronto, there's trash everywhere.
Well, it's on the countryside in all those other countries.
I have a minute and a half left.
This is the first time I've been interviewed by my own camera.
I'll have to, I'd like to get some contact information from you so that when you're done I can check out your, your YouTube things from all your holiday travels.
Okay, I'll just write that down there.
I don't know, it's gonna have a more interesting life.
Maybe I'll become one of your blog followers.
I'm gonna show Malvina my website when we get home.
I'll check it out as well.
Anyways, I think I'm out of time with that particular video.
